title: ANS Vocabulary
description: Essential terminology for autonomic nervous system divisions, neurotransmitters, and receptors

questions.q: The sympathetic nervous system is also known as the:
questions.explain: The sympathetic division prepares the body for stress — increasing heart rate, dilating airways, and diverting blood to muscles.

optionExplains.text: Rest and digest is parasympathetic — the calming division.
Fight or flight — sympathetic activation prepares you to confront or escape danger. Heart pounds, pupils dilate, digestion stops, airways open.
Somatic system controls voluntary skeletal muscle — not autonomic.
Enteric system is the gut's 'little brain' — part of ANS but not fight or flight.

questions.q: Sympathetic preganglionic neurons originate in which region of the spinal cord?
questions.explain: Sympathetic outflow is thoracolumbar — preganglionic cell bodies reside in the lateral horn from T1 to L2.

optionExplains.text: No sympathetic preganglionic neurons originate in cervical cord.
Thoracolumbar outflow — T1 to L2 lateral horns contain sympathetic preganglionic neurons. Their short axons synapse in paravertebral or prevertebral ganglia.
Sacral is parasympathetic territory (S2-S4).
Only thoracolumbar region, not the entire cord.

questions.q: The neurotransmitter released by all preganglionic autonomic neurons is:
questions.explain: All preganglionic neurons — both sympathetic and parasympathetic — release acetylcholine onto nicotinic receptors in ganglia.

optionExplains.text: Norepinephrine is released by most sympathetic POSTganglionic neurons.
Epinephrine is released by adrenal medulla, not preganglionic neurons.
Acetylcholine — both divisions use ACh at ganglionic synapses. The difference is what postganglionic neurons release at target organs.
Dopamine has limited autonomic roles — not the standard preganglionic neurotransmitter.

questions.q: Parasympathetic preganglionic fibers are described as:
questions.explain: Parasympathetic preganglionic fibers are LONG — they travel from brainstem or sacral cord to ganglia near or within target organs.

optionExplains.text: Short describes sympathetic preganglionic fibers (synapse nearby in chain ganglia).
Long preganglionic fibers — parasympathetic ganglia are near targets, so preganglionics travel far. The vagus nerve is a long preganglionic highway.
Preganglionic fibers are myelinated (type B fibers).
Preganglionic fibers definitely exist in parasympathetic division.

questions.q: The adrenal medulla releases primarily:
questions.explain: The adrenal medulla is modified sympathetic tissue releasing catecholamines — about 80% epinephrine, 20% norepinephrine — directly into blood.

optionExplains.text: ACh activates the medulla but isn't what it releases.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine — chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla are like postganglionic neurons that release into bloodstream instead of synapses.
Cortisol comes from adrenal CORTEX, not medulla.
Aldosterone is also adrenal cortex — mineralocorticoid hormone.

questions.q: Alpha-1 adrenergic receptors on blood vessels cause:
questions.explain: Alpha-1 receptors in blood vessel smooth muscle cause vasoconstriction when stimulated by norepinephrine — increasing blood pressure.

optionExplains.text: Vasodilation occurs with beta-2 receptors (in skeletal muscle vessels) or lack of sympathetic tone.
Vasoconstriction — alpha-1 activation contracts vascular smooth muscle. This shunts blood away from skin and gut during stress, toward muscles.
Heart rate is controlled by cardiac beta-1 receptors, not vascular alpha-1.
Bronchodilation is beta-2 receptor effect.

questions.q: The craniosacral outflow refers to the:
questions.explain: Parasympathetic is craniosacral — preganglionic neurons exit from brainstem (cranial nerves) and sacral spinal cord (S2-S4).

optionExplains.text: Sympathetic is thoracolumbar outflow.
Craniosacral outflow — cranial nerves III, VII, IX, X carry parasympathetic fibers; sacral segments S2-S4 supply pelvic organs.
Somatic motor exits throughout spinal cord — not specifically craniosacral.
Enteric system is intrinsic to the gut wall.

questions.q: Beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart increase:
questions.explain: Beta-1 receptors in cardiac muscle increase heart rate (chronotropy) and contraction strength (inotropy) when stimulated by catecholamines.

optionExplains.text: Heart rate and contractility — beta-1 receptors speed the SA node and strengthen ventricular contraction. Beta blockers target these to slow the heart.
Airway diameter is beta-2 receptor mediated.
Digestive activity is increased by parasympathetic, not beta-1.
Pupil constriction is parasympathetic (muscarinic).

questions.q: Nicotinic receptors are found in:
questions.explain: Nicotinic cholinergic receptors are ion channels found at all autonomic ganglia and at the somatic neuromuscular junction.

optionExplains.text: Heart has muscarinic receptors for parasympathetic control.
Ganglia and NMJ — nicotinic receptors are fast, ionotropic channels. ACh opens them directly. Ganglionic nicotinics relay autonomic signals; NMJ nicotinics trigger muscle contraction.
Smooth muscle has muscarinic receptors, not nicotinic.
Sweat glands have muscarinic receptors (unusual sympathetic exception).

questions.q: The vagus nerve provides parasympathetic innervation to:
options: Heart, lungs, and most digestive organs
questions.explain: The vagus nerve (CN X) is the primary parasympathetic pathway to thoracic and abdominal viscera — heart, lungs, stomach, intestines.

optionExplains.text: Lower limbs receive somatic innervation, not vagal parasympathetic.
Heart, lungs, digestive tract — the vagus 'wanders' from brainstem through neck, thorax, and abdomen. It slows heart, constricts airways, stimulates digestion.
Sweat glands are sympathetic (cholinergic but sympathetic).
Adrenal medulla receives sympathetic preganglionic fibers, not vagal.

questions.q: Muscarinic receptors are classified as:
questions.explain: Muscarinic receptors are metabotropic — G protein-coupled receptors that trigger second messenger cascades when ACh binds.

optionExplains.text: Ionotropic receptors are ion channels. Nicotinic is ionotropic; muscarinic isn't.
G protein-coupled (metabotropic) — muscarinic receptor activation triggers intracellular signaling cascades. Effects are slower but more sustained than nicotinic.
Voltage-gated channels respond to membrane potential, not neurotransmitters.
Ligand-gated ion channels are ionotropic — that's nicotinic, not muscarinic.

questions.q: Sympathetic stimulation causes pupils to:
questions.explain: Sympathetic activation dilates pupils (mydriasis) by contracting the dilator pupillae muscle. This lets more light in during danger.

optionExplains.text: Constriction is parasympathetic — sphincter pupillae contraction.
Mydriasis — pupil dilation. The radial dilator muscle contracts under sympathetic (alpha-1) stimulation. Wide pupils improve peripheral vision during threats.
Pupils definitely respond to autonomic input.
Oscillation (hippus) is pathological, not normal sympathetic effect.

questions.q: The sympathetic chain ganglia are also called:
questions.explain: Paravertebral ganglia form two chains alongside the vertebral column — connected sympathetic ganglia from cervical to sacral levels.

optionExplains.text: Terminal ganglia are parasympathetic — near or in target organs.
Paravertebral ganglia — the sympathetic chain or trunk. Preganglionic fibers enter, may synapse there or travel up/down to synapse at different levels.
Prevertebral ganglia are unpaired ganglia anterior to the aorta (celiac, superior mesenteric).
Dorsal root ganglia contain sensory neuron cell bodies — not autonomic.

questions.q: Acetylcholine is broken down by the enzyme:
questions.explain: Acetylcholinesterase rapidly hydrolyzes ACh in the synaptic cleft, terminating its action within milliseconds.

optionExplains.text: MAO breaks down monoamines like norepinephrine and serotonin.
Acetylcholinesterase — this enzyme cleaves ACh into acetate and choline. The choline is recycled. Nerve agents inhibit this enzyme, causing ACh accumulation.
COMT degrades catecholamines, not acetylcholine.
Tyrosine hydroxylase synthesizes catecholamines — doesn't break down ACh.

questions.q: Dual innervation means an organ receives input from:
questions.explain: Most visceral organs have dual innervation — both sympathetic and parasympathetic fibers providing opposing or complementary control.

optionExplains.text: That would be redundant sympathetic innervation, not dual.
Dual innervation — heart, GI tract, eyes, etc. Receive both divisions. Usually they oppose each other: Sympathetic speeds heart, parasympathetic slows it.
Somatic-autonomic would be different systems, not 'dual innervation' terminology.
Bilateral innervation is anatomical, not dual in ANS sense.

questions.q: Cholinergic fibers release:
questions.explain: Cholinergic means acetylcholine-releasing. All preganglionic fibers and parasympathetic postganglionic fibers are cholinergic.

optionExplains.text: Norepinephrine-releasing fibers are called adrenergic.
Acetylcholine — cholinergic fibers include all preganglionics, all parasympathetic postganglionics, and sympathetic fibers to sweat glands.
Epinephrine is released by adrenal medulla, not fibers.
Serotonin is a CNS and enteric neurotransmitter — not standard ANS.

questions.q: The celiac ganglion provides sympathetic innervation to:
questions.explain: The celiac ganglion is a prevertebral ganglion supplying upper abdominal organs — stomach, liver, spleen, kidneys.

optionExplains.text: Heart receives sympathetic from cervical and upper thoracic chain ganglia.
Stomach and liver — the celiac ganglion surrounds the celiac trunk. Postganglionic fibers follow arteries to reach upper abdominal viscera.
Bladder receives sympathetic from inferior hypogastric plexus.
Lungs receive sympathetic from upper thoracic chain ganglia.

questions.q: Beta-2 adrenergic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle cause:
questions.explain: Beta-2 receptors relax bronchial smooth muscle, dilating airways. This is why beta-2 agonists (albuterol) are used for asthma.

optionExplains.text: Bronchoconstriction is parasympathetic (muscarinic) or inflammatory.
Bronchodilation — beta-2 activation relaxes airway smooth muscle. During stress, you need more air. Asthma inhalers are beta-2 agonists.
Mucus secretion is glandular — influenced by parasympathetic.
Cough reflex is sensory — not a beta-2 effect.

questions.q: The sympathetic nervous system has:
options: Long preganglionic and short postganglionic fibers
Short preganglionic and long postganglionic fibers
questions.explain: Sympathetic preganglionics are short (synapse in nearby chain ganglia); postganglionics are long (travel to distant targets).

optionExplains.text: That describes parasympathetic — long preganglionics to distant ganglia.
Short preganglionics synapse in paravertebral or prevertebral ganglia close to the cord. Long postganglionics then travel to target organs throughout the body.
Fiber lengths differ significantly between the two neuron types.
Two-neuron chains (pre- and postganglionic) are universal in ANS.

questions.q: Parasympathetic stimulation of the GI tract causes:
questions.explain: Parasympathetic activation promotes digestion — increasing gut motility, glandular secretion, and relaxing sphincters.

optionExplains.text: Decreased activity is sympathetic — 'rest and digest' is parasympathetic.
Increased motility and secretion — parasympathetic is 'rest and digest.' The vagus nerve stimulates stomach acid, pancreatic enzymes, and intestinal churning.
Sphincters RELAX under parasympathetic control to allow material passage.
GI blood vessels dilate with parasympathetic activation.

questions.q: Adrenergic fibers release:
questions.explain: Adrenergic means norepinephrine-releasing. Most sympathetic postganglionic fibers are adrenergic.

optionExplains.text: ACh-releasing fibers are cholinergic.
Norepinephrine — adrenergic fibers are most sympathetic postganglionics. They activate alpha and beta receptors on target tissues.
Dopamine has limited peripheral autonomic role — primarily a CNS transmitter.
GABA is inhibitory CNS neurotransmitter, not peripheral ANS.

questions.q: The enteric nervous system is located in:
options: The wall of the digestive tract
questions.explain: The enteric nervous system — 'the gut brain' — is embedded in the GI tract wall, containing millions of neurons that regulate digestion locally.

optionExplains.text: Spinal cord is CNS, not where enteric neurons reside.
Digestive tract wall — the enteric nervous system spans esophagus to anus. Myenteric and submucosal plexuses control motility and secretion semi-independently.
Sympathetic ganglia are along the vertebral column, not in gut wall.
Brainstem influences GI via vagus, but enteric neurons are in the gut itself.

questions.q: Alpha-2 adrenergic receptors function mainly to:
questions.explain: Alpha-2 receptors are primarily presynaptic autoreceptors — when activated by norepinephrine, they inhibit further NE release (negative feedback).

optionExplains.text: Heart rate increase is beta-1 receptor mediated.
Inhibit norepinephrine release — alpha-2 receptors on presynaptic terminals sense NE levels and reduce further release. Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine) lower blood pressure this way.
Bronchodilation is beta-2 receptor function.
Pupil constriction is muscarinic (parasympathetic).

questions.q: Sweat glands are innervated by sympathetic fibers that release:
questions.explain: Eccrine sweat glands are a sympathetic exception — innervated by sympathetic fibers that are cholinergic (release ACh), not adrenergic.

optionExplains.text: Most sympathetic postganglionics release NE, but sweat glands are the exception.
Acetylcholine — sympathetic but cholinergic. These fibers activate muscarinic receptors on sweat glands. It's a common exam trick question.
Epinephrine comes from adrenal medulla, not sweat gland innervation.
Dopamine has minimal role in sweat gland control.

questions.q: The oculomotor nerve (CN III) carries parasympathetic fibers to:
questions.explain: CN III carries parasympathetic fibers that constrict the pupil (sphincter pupillae) and focus the lens (ciliary muscle) for near vision.

optionExplains.text: Salivary glands receive parasympathetic via CN VII and IX.
Heart receives parasympathetic via the vagus nerve (CN X).
Pupil constrictor and ciliary muscle — the Edinger-Westphal nucleus sends parasympathetic fibers via CN III to the ciliary ganglion, then to intrinsic eye muscles.
Lacrimal gland receives parasympathetic via CN VII (facial nerve).
